items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: An AO3 weakness of the Multi-Store Model is that:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: LTM has subtypes it ignores. “It is untestable” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which researcher is famous for studying leading questions and EWT?
items.why: It's Loftus. “Bartlett” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Peterson & Peterson found STM lasts roughly how many seconds without rehearsal (upper bound)?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
